cards.text: The process of building complex objects from simpler ones is called {{c1::object composition}}. There are two types of object composition: {{c2::composition}} and {{c3::aggregation}}.

cards.explanation:
Key differences between relationship types:
  • Composition: Part-of, whole/part, member can't belong to multiple classes, existence managed by class, unidirectional
  • Aggregation: Has-a, whole/part, member can belong to multiple classes, existence NOT managed by class, unidirectional
  • Association: Uses-a, otherwise unrelated, can belong to multiple classes, existence NOT managed, uni- or bidirectional
  • Dependency: Depends-on, otherwise unrelated, can belong to multiple classes, existence NOT managed, unidirectional

cards.question: An Animal class contains an animal type (enum) and name (string). What type of relationship is this?
cards.explanation: The animal type and name are part of the Animal and don't have a use outside of it. The Animal manages their existence completely.

cards.front: A text editor class has a save() function that takes a File object as an argument to write contents to disk. What type of relationship is this?
cards.back: Dependency - The text editor class is using the File object for the task of saving to disk, but the File is not a member.

cards.front: An Adventurer class can carry various Items (swords, potions, etc.). These Items can be dropped and picked up by other Adventurers. What type of relationship is this?
cards.back: Association - The Adventurer and Items aren't a whole-part relationship, so this isn't aggregation. The Adventurer uses-a Items, and doesn't manage their existence.

cards.front: A Player prays at a Shrine to receive a new power. What type of relationship is this?
cards.back: Dependency - The player depends on the shrine to receive the power, but does not have any long-term association with it.

cards.front: A Computer class contains a CPU class. The CPU can be removed from the Computer and tested on its own. What type of relationship is this?
cards.back: Aggregation - The computer has-a CPU, but does not manage its existence. The CPU can exist independently.

cards.front: A blacksmith has a specific anvil that he uses to do his work. What type of relationship is this?
cards.back: Association - The blacksmith uses the anvil, but does not have a whole-part relationship with it.

cards.text: If you can design a class using {{c1::composition}}, then you should use {{c1::composition}}.

cards.front: Which relationship types allow members to belong to multiple classes at the same time?
cards.back: Aggregation, association, and dependency all allow members to belong to multiple classes. Only composition restricts membership to a single class.

cards.front: Which relationship type has the class managing the existence of its members?
cards.back: Only composition. In aggregation, association, and dependency, the class does NOT manage the member's existence.

cards.front: Which relationship type can be bidirectional?
cards.back: Only association can be bidirectional (reflexive association). Composition, aggregation, and dependency are always unidirectional.

cards.text: The relationship type for composition and aggregation is {{c1::whole/part}}, while for association and dependency it is {{c2::otherwise unrelated}}.

cards.concept: How to choose the right relationship type
cards.explanation:
Decision process:
1. Is the object a part of the whole, with lifetime managed by it? → Composition
2. Is the object a part of the whole, but exists independently? → Aggregation
3. Does the object use another unrelated object with some persistence? → Association
4. Does the object use another object only for a specific task? → Dependency

Always prefer composition when possible.
examples: Person/Heart → Composition (heart can't exist without person)

cards.text: Only {{c1::composition}} restricts a member to belonging to a single object. {{c2::Aggregation}}, {{c3::association}}, and {{c4::dependency}} all allow sharing.
Only {{c1::composition}} has the class managing member existence. In {{c2::aggregation}}, {{c3::association}}, and {{c4::dependency}}, existence is NOT managed.
